analogy: Think of "Rights in the Indian Constitution" through the chapter's own opening images: 26 Indian Constitution at Work Chapter Two RIGHTS IN THE INDIAN CONSTITUTION INTRODUCTION A constitution is not only about the composition of the various organs of government and the relations among them. As we studied in the last chapter, the constitution is a document that... The easy idea is to follow the chapter's own clues one by one instead of memorising a loose definition. First notice the words and examples the text repeats: RIGHTS IN THE INDIAN, CONSTITUTION, INTRODUCTION, THE IMPORTANCE OF RIGHTS. Then connect each example to the question the chapter is trying to answer. Like solving a source-backed puzzle, every useful point must come from the chapter text itself and stay traceable to the NCERT source.
full_notes:
Rights in the Indian Constitution is decoded here only from the NCERT chapter text. The chapter begins with its own framing, examples and questions, then builds the topic through source-backed facts. The notes below keep that order: first the opening problem, then the important terms, then the mechanisms, examples and exam-useful connections that appear in the chapter itself.

The text explains that 26 Indian Constitution at Work Chapter Two RIGHTS IN THE INDIAN CONSTITUTION INTRODUCTION A constitution is not only about the composition of the various organs of government and the relations among them.

The text explains that as we studied in the last chapter, the constitution is a document that sets limits on the powers of the government and ensures a democratic system in which all persons enjoy certain rights.

The text explains that in this chapter, we shall study the Fundamental Rights contained in the Indian Constitution.

The text explains that part three of the Constitution of India lists the Fundamental Rights and also mentions the limits on these rights.

The text explains that in the past six decades, the scope of rights has changed and in some respects, expanded.

The text explains that after studying this chapter, you would know ± what are the various Fundamental Rights listed in the Constitution of India; ± how these rights are protected; ± what role the judiciary has played in protecting and interpreting these rights; a n d ± what is the difference between the Fundamental Rights and the Directive Principles of State Policy.

The text explains that 27 Chapter 2: Rights in the Indian Constitution THE IMPORTANCE OF RIGHTS In 1982 during the construction work for Asian Games the government engaged a few contractors.

The text explains that these contractors employed a large number of very poor construction workers from different parts of the country to build the flyovers and stadiums.

The text explains that these workers were kept in poor working conditions and were paid less than the minimum wages decided by the government.

The text explains that a team of social scientists studied their poor condition and petitioned the Supreme Court.

The text explains that they argued that employing a person to work for less than the minimum prescribed wage amounts to begar or forced labour, which is a violation of the Fundamental Right against exploitation.

The text explains that the court accepted this plea and directed the government to ensure that thousands of workers get the prescribed wages for their work.

The text explains that a resident of Chuburi village of Morigaon district of Assam, Machal was charged of causing grievous injuries.

The text explains that he was found mentally too unstable to stand trial and was sent as under trial to Lok Priya Gopinath Bordoloi Mental Hospital in Tejpur for treatment.

The text explains that machal was treated successfully and doctors wrote twice to jail authorities in 1967 and 1996 that he was fit to stand trial.

The text explains that machal Lalung remained in “judicial custody.’’ Machal Lalung was released in July 2005.

The text explains that he was freed when a team appointed by the National Human Rights Commission intervened after an inspection of undertrials in the State.

The text explains that our Constitution gives every citizen the right to ‘life and liberty’: this means that every citizen must also have the right to fair and speedy trial.

The text explains that machal’s case shows what happens when rights granted by the Constitution are not available in practice.

The text explains that 28 Indian Constitution at Work In the case of the first instance also there was violation of rights provided in the Constitution.

The text explains that as a result, workers could get what was due to them in the form of their rightful wages.

The text explains that the constitutional guarantee of the right against exploitation ensured justice to these workers.

The text explains that bill of Rights Both these examples show the importance of having rights and of the actual implementation of these rights.

The text explains that a democracy must ensure that individuals have certain rights and that the government will always recognise these rights.

The text explains that therefore it is often a practice in most democratic countries to list the rights of the citizens in the constitution itself.

The text explains that such a list of rights mentioned and protected by the constitution is called the ‘bill of rights’.

The text explains that a bill of rights prohibits government from thus acting against the rights of the individuals and ensures a remedy in case there is violation of these rights.

The text explains that from whom does a constitution protect the rights of the individual?

The text explains that the rights of a person may be threatened by another person or private organisation.

The text explains that in such a situation, the individual would need the protection of the government.

The text explains that so, it is necessary that the government is bound to protect the rights of the individual.

The text explains that on the other hand, the organs of the government (the legislature, executive, bureaucracy or even the judiciary), in the course of their functioning, may violate the rights of the person.

The text explains that fUNDAMENTAL RIGHTS IN THE INDIAN CONSTITUTION During our freedom struggle, the leaders of the freedom movement had realised the importance of rights and demanded that the British rulers should respect rights of the people.

The text explains that the Motilal Nehru committee had demanded a bill of rights as far back as in 1928.

The text explains that it was therefore, natural that when India became independent and the Constitution was being prepared, there were no I get it!

The text explains that the bill of rights is like a warrantee card that we get when we purchase a TV or a fan.

The text explains that 29 Chapter 2: Rights in the Indian Constitution Bill of rights in the South African Constitution The South African Constitution was inaugurated in December 1996.

The text explains that its creation and promulgation took place at a time when South Africa still faced the threat of a civil war after the dissolution of the Apartheid government.

The text explains that the South African Constitution says that its “Bill of Rights is a cornerstone of democracy in South Africa”.

The text explains that it grants perhaps the most extensive range of rights to the citizens.

The text explains that a special constitutional court enforces the rights enshrined in the constitution.

The text explains that some of the Rights included in the constitution of South Africa include: ± Right to Dignity ± Right to Privacy ± Right to fair labour practices ± Right to healthy environment and right to protection of environment ± Right to adequate housing ± Right to health care, food, water and social security ± Children’s rights ± Right to basic and higher education ± Right of cultural, religious and linguistic communities ± Right to information two opinions on the inclusion and protection of rights in the Constitution.

The text explains that which of their Fundamental Rights are being violated in this instance?

The text explains that a petition by a human rights group drew attention of the court to the condition of starvation and hunger in the country.

The text explains that over five crore tonnes of food grains was stored in the godowns of the Food Corporation of India.

The text explains that research shows that a large number of ration cardholders do not know about the quantity of food grains they can purchase from fair price shops.

The text explains that it requested the court to order the government to improve its public distribution system. a.

The text explains that which different rights does this case involve?

The text explains that should these rights form part of the right to life?

The text explains that read the statement by Somnath Lahiri in the Constitutent Assembly quoted in this chapter.

The text explains that which of the Fundamental Rights is in your opinion the most important right?

The text explains that summarise its provisions and give arguments to show why it is most important.

Source: NCERT Class 11 Indian Constitution at Work, Chapter 2: Rights in the Indian Constitution

prelims_traps: Do not treat RIGHTS IN THE INDIAN and CONSTITUTION as identical unless the source text links them directly.
Do not add outside examples to Rights in the Indian Constitution; use only the examples and processes named in the chapter.
Do not confuse a visible surface result with the internal process or source evidence described by the text.
mains_angles: Explain Rights in the Indian Constitution through the source chain: evidence, process and visible result.
Use RIGHTS IN THE INDIAN and CONSTITUTION to show how the chapter connects facts with mechanisms.
Frame an answer around causes, internal working, surface expression and source limitations from the NCERT chapter.
